AKU/AKHST 2026Scientific Conference · DSM
4–5 June 2026 · Dar es Salaam
Serena Hotel, Dar es Salaam
CPD Points · MCT Compliant
Workshops — Day 1

Hands-on pre-conference training · 4 June 2026 · Tap a workshop for details

🚑
From Panic to Prepared
Practical First Aid for First Responders · Room 1
▼
FacilitatorsDr. Hussein K. Manji & Dr. Elishah Premji
AudienceAll healthcare personnel, all cadres
RoomKivukoni 1
Content
1.Initial approach to an unresponsive victim
2.Primary survey in emergencies
3.CPR in adults, children and infants
4.Management of choking in adults, children and infants
5.Tips and tricks for first aid in common emergencies
💻
Future-Proofing Healthcare
Digital Innovation & Climate Resilience · Room 2
▼
🌍 Climate Change & Health Systems
FacilitatorGeorge Humba — GRI Certified Sustainability Professional
Content
1.Climate change and health systems: risks, pathways, and impacts
2.Assessing vulnerability and climate risks in health systems
3.Practical approaches to building climate-resilient health services
4.Sustainable and low-carbon health system interventions
5.Action planning and monitoring: integrating climate resilience into health system strengthening
🤖 From Data to Diagnosis — AI in Healthcare
FacilitatorDr. Deogratias Mzurikwao — ETH Lab, MUHAS
FormatInteractive — bring a laptop or tablet
Content
1.Why AI in Healthcare Now — and why it matters more in LMICs
2.How AI Actually Works — demystifying ML, deep learning, and neural networks
3.AI in Clinical Practice — live demos: echocardiography, Pap smear, TB screening
4.Ethical, regulatory & equity dimensions — Tanzania TMDA, data governance, algorithmic bias
RoomRuaha Conference Room
👶
Saving Small Lives
Essential Skills in Neonatal Resuscitation · Room 3
▼
FacilitatorsDr. Yasser Abdallah & Dr. Naomi Mwamanenge
AudienceAll healthcare personnel
RoomKivukoni 3
Content
1.Preparation for newborn delivery
2.The golden minute
3.Effective ventilation and chest compression
4.Post resuscitation care
🎓
Teaching Tomorrow's Health Workers
Teaching Skills for Medical Educators · Room 4
▼
FacilitatorsDr. Mariam Noorani & Dr. Nahida Walli
AudienceMedical educators and clinical trainers
RoomKivukoni 2
Content
1.Effective teaching in the clinical setting — wards, clinics and operating theatres
2.Teaching using simulation
3.Teaching large classes and small group sessions
4.Using Artificial Intelligence in teaching and learning
5.Designing effective assessments
📋
Beyond the Checklist
Practical Skills for Measuring Healthcare Quality · Room 5
▼
FacilitatorsDr. Amri Kyaruzi Ishengoma, Emmanuel Mongi & Aminieli Usiri
AudienceHealth professionals, quality officers, clinical managers
RoomRuvu Conference Room
Content
1.Developing meaningful healthcare quality indicators
2.Sources of quality related data
3.Analyzing and interpreting healthcare quality data effectively
4.Utilising tools for quality improvement
5.The patient's perspective as a quality measure
💊
Making Medications Safer
Excellence in Medication Management Systems · Room 6
▼
FacilitatorsVaidehi Bhatia (Clinical Pharmacist) & Charity Alphonce (EHR Pharmacist)
AudiencePharmacists, nurses, clinicians, health informatics professionals
RoomRufiji Conference Room
Content
1.Quality manufacturing, governance & access to medications
2.Safe prescribing and administration
3.Medicine reconciliation and dispensing
4.Data and informatics in medication management
5.Pharmacovigilance and antimicrobial stewardship
Our Sponsors
💎 Diamond
🥇 Gold
AKU/AKHST 2026

A Welcome Message
from Our Leadership

Prof. Paschal Ruggajo
Vice Dean, AKU Medical College Tanzania
We are delighted to welcome you to the Aga Khan Hospital and Aga Khan University Scientific Conference 2026. Your presence reflects a shared commitment to advancing healthcare quality, innovation, research, and medical education in Tanzania and beyond. As you engage in the workshops, presentations, and discussions, we hope this conference inspires collaboration, meaningful learning, and practical solutions that will strengthen patient care in our communities. Thank you for being part of this important gathering. We wish you a productive, insightful, and enjoyable conference experience.
Mr. Sisawo Konteh
CEO, Aga Khan Health Service, Tanzania
It is my great pleasure to warmly welcome you to this landmark joint conference. This conference, themed "Leveraging Quality Healthcare in the Health Sector," reflects our shared vision to strengthen collaboration, exchange experiences, and collectively elevate healthcare standards across Tanzania and beyond. We are honoured to host healthcare professionals, educators, researchers, policymakers, and practitioners who are united by a common purpose: building high-quality, patient-centred, and resilient health systems for our communities. Over the course of this conference, I trust that the workshop sessions and scientific discussions on quality improvement, healthcare leadership, education and research, digital health, innovation, and climate resilience will inspire meaningful learning and practical action. Together, these engagements will foster a strong culture of quality within our hospitals and institutions while embracing sustainable and green hospital initiatives that will shape the future of healthcare delivery. This conference will also serve as a platform for building stronger partnerships that will help position Tanzanian hospitals among the region's leading quality-oriented healthcare facilities and beyond. I wish you productive deliberations, engaging discussions, and a successful conference.
Conference Theme
"Leveraging Quality Health Care in Tanzania: From Evidence to Excellence"
4–5 June 2026 · Serena Hotel, Dar es Salaam

← Abstracts & Presenters
45
Total presentations
36 Oral · 9 Poster
Day 2 · 5 June 2026
Theme 1: Quality Improvement and Patient Safety in Clinical Practice
10:30–11:45 · Kivukoni 1 · Chair: Aminieli Usiri · Rapporteur: Dr. Daisy Mhando · Judges: Prof. Daudi Simba & Aminieli Usiri
Effectiveness of Innovated Eye Shield for phototherapy compared to improvised materials and imported eye shield: An Experimental Study
Consolata Matee · Doctor · MUHAS
Co-authors: Dr. Satrumin Shirima, Dr. Gloria Mrosso, Mr. Omari Pinda, Dr. Evelyne Assenga
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Neonatal jaundice accounts for 21.99% of all neonatal admissions globally, with South-East Asia and Africa having a high prevalence of 30.61% and 20.10%, respectively; approximately 14.26% develop severe jaundice. Phototherapy remains the main treatment, but is associated with retinal damage and permanent vision loss if eyes are inadequately shielded. Many low-income settings use improvised materials like gauze despite uncertainty regarding their effectiveness in shielding and reducing neonatal discomfort
Objectives: To assess the effectiveness of the Innovated Eye Shield for phototherapy, and compare it with improvised materials (gauze) and imported eye shield
Methods: An experimental comparative study measured irradiance transmission through three materials: gauze, an imported eye shield, and the Innovated Eye Shield for phototherapy. Testing used a double-sided LED phototherapy device (Firefly) at 15 cm. A custom-designed testing board with three cut-outs positioned materials uniformly. Irradiance was measured using an MTTS light meter with upper and lower-sensor pairs. Baseline irradiance was first measured without any protective material, followed by measurements for each material
Results: The control setup without eye protection demonstrated a total irradiance of 64 µW/cm²/nm. The Innovated Eye Shield for phototherapy completely blocked irradiance transmission to the lower sensor (0 µW/cm²/nm), with an upper sensor reading of 52 µW/cm²/nm, comparable to the imported eye shield, which also demonstrated complete lower sensor blockage (0 µW/cm²/nm). In contrast, gauze allowed partial light transmission, with a lower sensor irradiance of 4 µW/cm²/nm and total irradiance of 59 µW/cm²/nm
Conclusion: The Innovated Eye Shield for phototherapy demonstrated effective protection against phototherapy light exposure comparable to imported eye shields, and was superior to gauze commonly used in local settings. This device can be adopted in low-resource settings, and its reusability after sterilization makes it a cost-effective intervention
Risk Factors and Clinical Outcomes in Hospitalized Patients with Multi Drug Resistant Organism (MDRO) Infections admitted in a Tertiary Hospital in Tanzania
Samina Somji · Doctor · AKU/AKH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Antimicrobial resistance is rising globally, and Sub-Saharan Africa has shown the highest mortality rate due to infections with Multidrug-Resistant Organisms (MDRO). This study aimed to identify risk factors and clinical outcomes of MDRO infections among hospitalized patients in Aga Khan Hospital, Dar-es-Salaam, Tanzania
Objectives: This study aimed to identify risk factors and clinical outcomes of MDRO infections among hospitalized patients in Aga Khan Hospital, Dar-es-Salaam, Tanzania
Methods: This retrospective analytical cross-sectional study included patients’ ≥ 18 years old admitted to the Aga Khan Hospital, Dar-es-Salaam between 1st January 2021 and 31st December 2023 with positive microbial cultures. Study sample data was extracted from the laboratory on all positive cultures as a master frame
Results: 398 patients were included, comprising 211/398 (53.0%) males. The prevalence of MDRO infection was 62.5% (248/398). The commonest MDRO group was ESBL (112/212, 52.8%) and organism was E. coli (57/249, 22.9%). MDRO infection was associated with older age (OR=1.70,p=0.035), use of acid-suppressive drugs (OR=1.69,p=0.023), prolonged length of stay (LOS) (OR=4.01,p<0.001), cultures taken >3 days of admission (OR=2.04,p=0.007), genitourinary tract (GUT) infection as source of infection (OR=2.68,p=0.01), use of medical devices like NGT (OR=1.79,p<0.042), endotracheal tube (OR=2.25,p<0.019), central venous line (OR=3.9,p<0.003), arterial line (OR=3.79,p<0.035), and Charlson morbidity index of ≥ 3 (OR=1.78,p=<0.007). Multivariable analysis showed prolonged LOS (OR=4.01, p=0.001) and GUT infection (OR=2.68,p=0.05) were strongly associated with MDRO infections
Conclusion: This study identified a high prevalence of MDRO infections. The LOS and source of infection were strongly associated with MDRO infections. Stringent infection control practices and targeted interventions are important in these risky groups requiring continued surveillance and robust antibiotic stewardship programs to prevent and manage MDRO infections
Enhancing Quality Improvement in IVF Practice Through Effective Quality Control in the Embryology Laboratory
Praveena · Embryologist · Nulife Advanced Fertility Centre
Co-authors: Mr. Muralidhar Babu CV, Dr. Madhav Hirani, Dr. Sheila Karim R
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Quality control in the embryology laboratory is a critical component of successful in vitro fertilization (IVF), as embryo development is highly sensitive to environmental and procedural conditions. Factors such as incubator stability, culture media conditions, air quality, temperature regulation, and standardized embryo handling directly influence fertilization, cleavage, and blastocyst development. Maintaining consistent laboratory practices is therefore essential to ensure reproducible embryology outcomes and optimize IVF success. Evaluation of embryo developmental parameters can serve as an indirect indicator of laboratory performance and the effectiveness of quality control measures in assisted reproductive technology (ART)
Objectives: To evaluate embryology laboratory performance as an indicator of quality control effectiveness in IVF practice using key developmental parameters, including fertilization, cleavage, blastocyst formation, and embryo utilisation outcomes across IVF cycles
Methods: This retrospective observational study was conducted at Nulife Advanced Fertility Centre. A total of 226 IVF cycles were analyzed using routine embryology laboratory records. Embryo developmental outcomes from fertilization to blastocyst stage were assessed. Standardized embryology protocols, including controlled culture conditions, incubator maintenance, and consistent embryo handling procedures, were followed throughout the study period. Primary outcome measures included fertilization, cleavage, blastocyst formation, and embryo utilisation rates
Results: A total of 226 IVF cycles were analyzed, resulting in 1306 embryos formed during the study period. The embryology laboratory demonstrated a fertilization rate of 76%, cleavage rate of 90%, blastocyst formation rate of 70%, and embryo utilisation rate of 55%. These findings indicate consistent embryo developmental progression and stable laboratory performance under standardized culture conditions
Conclusion: Consistent fertilization, cleavage, blastocyst formation, and embryo utilisation outcomes reflect stable embryology laboratory performance under standardized culture conditions. Continuous monitoring, adherence to quality control measures, regular equipment maintenance, and standardized laboratory protocols are essential to support reliable embryo development and sustained quality improvement in IVF practice
Effectiveness of a Structured Handover Checklist in the Post-Anesthesia Care Unit on Reducing Information Loss and Complication Rates at Muhimbili National Hospital: A Pre-Post Implementation Study
Shivangi Mandania · Resident · MUHAS
Co-authors: Dr. Respicious Boniface, Dr. Moses Mulungu, Dr. Mpoki Ulisubisya
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Effective communication during patient handover is critical for ensuring patient safety in the post-anesthesia care unit (PACU). However, unstructured handover practices are often associated with information loss and increased risk of complications
Objectives: To assess the effectiveness of a structured handover checklist in reducing information loss and complication rates at post-anesthesia care unit in Muhimbili National Hospital, from May 2025 to November 2025
Methods: A prospective pre–post interventional study was conducted among 332 patients, with 166 patients in each phase, at the Main Operating Theater. Baseline handover practices were assessed before implementation of the checklist, followed by staff training and post-implementation evaluation. Data obtained was analyzed using SPSS (Version 26). Continuous variables were summarized using mean (standard deviation) or median (interquartile range), as appropriate. Categorical variables were presented as frequencies and percentages. Associations between variables were assessed using Pearson’s Chi-square test. Complication rates were calculated with 95% confidence intervals, and odds ratios were estimated to compare groups. A p-value of <0.05 was considered statistically significant. Multivariable logistic regression was performed to identify independent predictors among variables found to be significant
Results: Implementation of the checklist resulted in a significant improvement in handover completeness, with good handovers increasing from 10% to 81.3% (p < 0.001). The proportion of patients developing complications decreased from 32% in the pre-implementation phase to 18% in the post-implementation phase (p = 0.004). Patients in the post-implementation phase had 48% lower odds of complications compared to the pre-implementation phase. Furthermore, good handover completeness was independently associated with a marked reduction in complications (adjusted OR = 0.06, 95% CI: 0.02–0.17, p < 0.001). The duration of handover was not significantly increased following checklist implementation
Conclusion: The use of a structured handover checklist significantly improved handover completeness and reduced complication rates without increasing handover duration. These findings support the integration of standardized handover tools into routine clinical practice to enhance patient safety in the PACU
Improving Quality of Clinicians handover using the ISBAR tool in the Paediatric and Neonatal units at a Tertiary hospital in Tanzania
Tatenda Magodi · Resident · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Clinical handovers are high risk periods for patient safety and can result in delay in treatment, incorrect medications, and unnecessary repetition of investigations, discontinuing care, adverse clinical events and legal claims of malpractice. An audit of our departmental key performance indicators and documented patient complaints identified clinical handover a contributing factor to, below benchmark performance and patient related complaints
Objectives: The study was conducted at the Aga Khan Hospital Dar es Salaam Paediatric and Neonatal units from March 2024 to July 2025. Using the PDSA framework we; a) assessed the current clinical handover practice of clinicians in the units (pre-intervention), b) educated the clinicians on implementing the ISBAR handover checklist model to improve on identified gaps (intervention), c) evaluated on the success of the interventions and improvement of handovers (post intervention)
Methods: The ISBAR tool was divided into 15 point checklist questions with a final Global rating score out of 5 which were assessed anonymously during bedside patient handovers in the pre-intervention and post intervention stages. 50 pre-intervention and 50 post intervention handovers were assessed using the 15 point tool and Global rating scores. Training involved a 30 minute lecture and review, videos and group practice of case scenarios
Results: There was a significant improvement in 7 out of 15 of the ISBAR tool parameters (p<0.05), (95%CI), and a 30% improvement in the overall Global rating score. Feedback from training was positive and we recommend regular sessions, audits, flashcards, reminders and training for new staff joining the department
Conclusion: A formal training of patient handover, structured approach, regular evaluation and reminders improves Quality of Clinicians’ handovers reduces risk of adverse events and improves their confidence in giving and receiving handover
Safe Phlebotomy Practice and Adherence to Infection Prevention and Control SOPs among Health Laboratory Practitioners in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Shekha Silim · Laboratory Technologist · MUHAS
Co-authors: Masatu Jonhas
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Safe phlebotomy remains a key part of infection prevention and control (IPC) within healthcare settings, especially in laboratory units where routine handling of blood places both patients and healthcare workers at increased risk of healthcare-associated infections. Although Tanzania has established national IPC standard operating procedures (SOPs), there is still limited evidence on how well these guidelines are followed in everyday practice by health laboratory practitioners. This study, therefore, assessed the level of adherence to safe phlebotomy practices and compliance among practitioners working in regional referral hospitals in Dar es Salaam
Objectives: This study aimed to assess the level of adherence to safe phlebotomy practices and compliance among practitioners working in regional referral hospitals in Dar es Salaam
Methods: A cross-sectional study with an observational approach was conducted in three regional referral hospitals: Amana, Mwananyamala, and Temeke hospitals. A total of 303 phlebotomy procedures were directly observed using a structured checklist adapted from the Tanzania National IPC Standards for Hospitals (2020). Descriptive analysis was used to summarize data in frequencies and percentages, and the Chi-square test was applied to examine the statistical differences between adherence and selected variables, with statistical significance set at p < 0.05
Results: Overall adherence to IPC SOPs was observed in 26(8.6%) procedures meeting the predefined compliance threshold. Adherence was consistently high for the technical steps of blood collection, use of sterile gloves, application of tourniquets, skin disinfection with alcohol swabs, and safe transportation of specimens, each observed in 303(100%) procedures. Correct use of single-use sampling instruments was noted in 301(99.3%) procedures, while proper patient identification was observed in 287 (94.7%). In contrast, Hand hygiene was performed before the procedure in 59 (19.5%) and after the procedure in just 13 (4.3%). Proper cleaning of the puncture site from the center outward was observed in 41 (13.5%), and only 127 (41.9%) allowed the site to dry before needle insertion. Adherence was significantly associated with professional designation (χ², p = 0.031) and the number of IPC trainings attended (χ², p = 0.020)
Conclusion: Adherence to safe phlebotomy practices in line with IPC SOPs was generally low. These findings highlight the need for regular refresher training, stronger supportive supervision, and routine observational audits to improve compliance and strengthen patient safety within laboratory settings
Theme 2: Patient-Centred Care, Access and Equity for Vulnerable Populations
10:30–11:45 · Kivukoni 2 · Chair: Dr. Daudi Gidion · Rapporteur: Dr. Caren Mussa · Judges: Dr. Yaser Abdallah & Dr. Daudi Gidion
Exploration of the Impact of Reconstructive Surgery on the Quality of Life of Women with Burn Related Deformities in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study
Nobert Kasinya · Resident · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Burn-related deformities are a major cause of long-term disability among women in low-resource settings, where access to reconstructive and rehabilitative care remains limited. In Tanzania, approximately 40% of burns occur within domestic kitchen environments. Despite advances in reconstructive surgery, little is known about how women experience recovery beyond surgical outcomes. In this qualitative study involving 10 Tanzanian women with burn-related deformities, recovery was primarily described through restoration of function, independence, caregiving ability, and social participation rather than cosmetic improvement alone. Participants further identified stigma, financial limitations, family support, and healthcare access as major determinants of long-term recovery
Objectives: To explore how reconstructive surgery influences the quality of life of Tanzanian women living with burn-related deformities, with particular emphasis on functional recovery, psychological well-being, social reintegration, autonomy, and the contextual factors shaping long-term postoperative recovery
Methods: A qualitative descriptive study was conducted at Aga Khan Hospital, Dar es Salaam, involving 10 women who had undergone reconstructive surgery for burn-related deformities. Participants were purposively sampled and interviewed using semi-structured in-depth interviews conducted in Swahili or English. Data were audio-recorded, transcribed verbatim, translated where necessary, and analysed using Braun and Clarke’s reflexive thematic analysis. The WHOQOL-BREF domains informed interview development, while interpretation was guided by the International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health framework
Results: Five major themes emerged among the 10 participants; a) Functional restoration as the primary pathway to regained autonomy, b) Recovery as negotiated improvement rather than complete restoration, c) Psychological recovery as reconstruction of self-perception, d) Social reintegration as restoration of participation and relational identity, e) Environmental conditions shaping the extent of lived recovery. Participants valued reconstructive surgery primarily through restored function, independence, caregiving ability, dignity, and social participation rather than cosmetic improvement alone
Conclusion: Women interpreted reconstructive surgery primarily through restoration of bodily function, autonomy, caregiving capacity, psychological confidence, and social participation rather than cosmetic improvement alone
Recommendation: Burn reconstruction services should integrate rehabilitation, psychosocial support, stigma-sensitive counselling, and structured long-term follow-up, while future longitudinal studies should evaluate sustained functional, psychological, and social outcomes after reconstruction
Early Breastfeeding Challenges and Associated Risk Factors among First-Time Mothers in Dar Es Salaam & Pwani Regions of Tanzania
Neema Balige · Doctor · MUHAS
Co-authors: Dr Mariam Noorani, Dr Rehema Enock
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months is vital for child health, but many mother-infant pairs struggle due to challenges like sore nipples, engorgement, mastitis, latching issues, and low milk supply. There is limited evidence of the magnitude and associated risk factors of these challenges in our setting
Objectives: To assess the prevalence and factors associated with early breastfeeding challenges among first-time mothers in Dar es Salaam and Pwani, Tanzania
Methods: This is a cross-sectional study with longitudinal follow-up, conducted from December 2024 to June 2025 in selected health facilities, involved 383 first-time mothers. Data was gathered through interviews within 24 hours of birth and follow-ups by phone at weeks 1, 4, and 6, using structured questionnaires. Frequencies described the categorical data, and logistic regression for correlated data identified factors associated with breastfeeding challenges
Results: Of the 383 mothers, 174 (45%) experienced early breastfeeding challenges, mostly within the first week, with perceived low milk supply being most common. Mothers with no or primary education and secondary education had lower odds (AOR 0.41 and AOR 0.37, respectively) of challenges compared to those with a college education. Single/divorced women also had lower odds (AOR 0.68). Those with low breastfeeding self-efficacy (AOR 4.74) and who lacked verbal advice on demand feeding (AOR 1.48) faced higher odds of challenges. Among those with challenges, 18% did not exclusively breastfeed versus 1% without challenges
Conclusion: Nearly half of first-time mothers face breastfeeding challenges within 24 hours, primarily regarding low milk supply. Higher education, marriage, low self-efficacy, and lack of breastfeeding counselling increase this risk. To reduce these challenges, especially for those with low self-efficacy, breastfeeding counselling during antenatal and postnatal care is essential
The Efficacy and Safety of Greater Occipital Nerve Block for Migraine in Pregnancy: A Systematic Review
Lusajo Mwagobele · Resident · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Migraine is a common neurological disorder among women of reproductive age and may persist or worsen during pregnancy. Pharmacological treatment options are limited by concerns regarding fetal safety, creating a need for effective non-systemic therapies. Greater occipital nerve block (GONB) has demonstrated efficacy in the general migraine population, but its role in pregnancy remains unclear
Objectives: To systematically evaluate the efficacy and safety of the greater occipital nerve block for the treatment of migraine in pregnant women
Methods: A systematic review was conducted in accordance with PRISMA 2020 guidelines. MEDLINE (via PubMed), Embase, CINAHL, Scopus, Cochrane CENTRAL, and Google Scholar were searched from inception to November 2025 with no language restrictions. Randomized and non-randomized studies evaluating GONB in pregnant women with migraine were included. Outcomes of interest included pain reduction, need for rescue medication, maternal adverse effects, obstetric complications, and neonatal outcomes. Risk of bias was assessed using RoB-2 and ROBINS-I tools. Data were synthesized narratively
Results: Four studies (n=164 pregnant women) met inclusion criteria: one randomized controlled trial, two retrospective cohort studies, and one conference abstract. GONB was associated with a rapid reduction in pain intensity and decreased need for rescue medications. No serious maternal, obstetric, or neonatal adverse events attributable to the intervention were reported. Study heterogeneity and limited sample sizes precluded meta-analysis
Conclusion: Available evidence suggests that greater occipital nerve block may be an effective and safe treatment option for migraine during pregnancy. However, the quality and quantity of evidence are limited (GRADE certainty: very low to low), highlighting the need for well-designed prospective studies
Exploring Quality of Life in Postoperative Rheumatic Heart Disease Patients at Jakaya Kikwete Cardiac Institute (JKCI)
Magreth Justine · Nurse · JKCI
Co-authors: Dr Loveluck Mwasha
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Rheumatic Heart Disease (RHD) continues to be a leading cause of cardiovascular morbidity and mortality, where the prevalence is estimated at approximately 9% among the general population in Tanzania. While surgical interventions are critical for advanced RHD, evaluations have largely focused on clinical outcomes, overlooking patients’ long-term, post-operative quality of life (QoL)
Objectives: To explore the lived experiences and perceptions of postoperative RHD patients at JKCI
Methods: This qualitative descriptive study employed an exploratory design to gain insight into postoperative QoL among RHD patients. Purposive sampling was used to recruit patients. Data was collected through in-depth, semi-structured interviews conducted at JKCI. Interviews were audio-recorded, transcribed verbatim, and analyzed using thematic analysis to identify recurring themes and patterns relevant to patients' lived experiences post-surgery
Results: a) Physical relief and burden-Patients reported significant symptom improvement and gratitude for life-saving surgery, but faced lifelong burden of anticoagulation management, dietary restrictions, and monthly International Normalized Ratio (INR) monitoring costs. Psychological distress, persistent anxiety, fear of re-operation, sudden complications, and death were common. b) Financial hardship-Participants struggled with lifelong medication costs, regular clinic follow-ups, and INR testing expenses, and c) Barriers to continuity of care-Fragmented communication between specialists and primary care providers, and limited access to essential medications and testing
Conclusion: Cardiac surgery for RHD offers essential physical relief but marks the start of a lifelong, complex journey for patients. Surgical success should therefore be redefined to include not only clinical outcomes but also a patient-centred support framework. To enhance long-term QoL, an integrated care model combining psychological support, financial assistance, and accessible post-operative services is essential
Understanding Barriers Associated with Delays in Diagnosis and Treatment among Colorectal Cancer Patients Treated at a Tertiary-Level Care Hospital – Tanzania
Mike Kimambo · Resident · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is a leading global cause of morbidity and mortality, with higher incidence in high-income regions but disproportionate mortality in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) due to late presentation and limited access to care. In LMICs like Tanzania, delays in diagnosis and treatment arise from patient, provider, and system-level factors, including low awareness, sociocultural beliefs, financial constraints, and poor healthcare accessibility. Limited diagnostic resources and high treatment costs further worsen outcomes. This study explores patient-perceived factors contributing to delays in CRC care in Tanzania using a qualitative approach and the Three Delays Model to inform context-specific interventions
Objectives: To understand perceived barriers associated with delays in diagnosis and treatment among colorectal cancer patients undergoing treatment at a tertiary-level care hospital in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Methods: This descriptive qualitative study was conducted at Aga Khan Hospital, Dar es Salaam, involving purposively selected colorectal cancer patients aged ≥18 years. Sample size was determined by data saturation. Data were collected through in-depth interviews using a semi-structured guide, conducted in English or Swahili, audio-recorded, and transcribed verbatim. Thematic analysis was performed iteratively. Credibility was ensured through triangulation, audit trails, and supervisor review. Ethical approval and informed consent were obtained, ensuring confidentiality and voluntary participation
Results: Nine participants (aged 48–70 years) were included, with most presenting at advanced stages. Delays occurred across patient, diagnostic, and treatment phases. Six major themes emerged: fragmented referral pathways, poor health-seeking behavior and mistrust, limited awareness of colorectal cancer, economic constraints, low healthcare provider capacity, and poor quality of life outcomes. Delays were driven by symptom normalization, misdiagnosis, limited diagnostic access, financial barriers, and systemic inefficiencies, leading to advanced-stage presentation and adverse psychosocial impact
Conclusion: Delays in colorectal cancer diagnosis and treatment in Tanzania are multifactorial, driven by limited awareness, fragmented referral systems, missed diagnostic opportunities, financial constraints, and sociocultural factors. Addressing these requires targeted interventions including public education, provider training, improved referral systems, and financial protection strategies to enhance early detection, timely treatment, and patient outcomes
Clinical-Pathological Profile and Survival Outcomes of Pediatric Primary Brain Tumours: A Five-Year Tertiary Hospital Review in Tanzania
Chambega Chambega · Doctor · MUHAS
Co-authors: Martha Mkony, Hamisi Shabani, Lulu Chirande
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Paediatric brain tumours remain the second most common type of malignancy in children. While advancements in high-income countries (HICs) allow survival rates as high as 70%, little is known about the burden of disease in low and middle-income countries (LMICs). Notably, in sub-Saharan Africa, fewer than 50% of these children survive, highlighting a disparity in the outcomes of these patients
Objectives: This study aimed to determine the clinical profile, treatment modalities and survival outcomes of children with primary brain tumours managed at a tertiary hospital in Tanzania
Methods: This was a retrospective review of a cohort of patients aged 0–18 years with histologically confirmed primary brain tumours managed between January 2020 and December 2024. Data collected from their medical records included their clinical presentation, treatment modalities, histopathology report and follow-up status post-treatment. Survival probabilities were estimated using Kaplan-Meier analysis
Results: A total of 74 patients were identified, with a mean age of 7.2 years at diagnosis. The median duration of symptoms before admission was 5 months. The most common presenting symptoms were headache, visual impairment, nausea & vomiting, and unsteady gait, respectively. Most patients underwent surgery (88%), with subtotal resection being the most common outcome (62%). Postoperative complications occurred in 38% of patients, primarily respiratory failure and seizures, with a 30-day post-surgery mortality of 18.5%. The most common histological subtypes were astrocytomas (30%), medulloblastoma (23%) and craniopharyngiomas (17%), respectively. Overall survival (OS) at 1 and 2 years was 52% and 37.8%, respectively. Astrocytoma histology, gross total resections (GTR) and female patients were associated with better survival, while brainstem tumours had the poorest outcome
Conclusion: Paediatric brain tumour care in this setting is characterised by delayed presentation and low overall survival rates. These findings highlight the critical need for enhanced diagnostic capacity and improved access to comprehensive multidisciplinary paediatric oncology care in the region
Theme 3: Health Systems Strengthening and Workforce Development
10:30–11:45 · Kivukoni 3 · Chair: Dr. Ritha Ako · Rapporteur: Dr. Edward Kijazi · Judges: Prof. Paschal Ruggajo & Dr. Ritha Ako
Introducing Family Medicine in Tanzania: Strengthening Primary Health Care through the 5 Cs - Advocacy through a Deliberative Dialogue Symposium-A case study of Multinational Academic Collaboration
Dr. Nancy Matillya · Family Physician · Aga Khan University
Co-authors: Donatus Rutajama Mutasingwa, Enica Richard, Matilda Alfred, and 14 others
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Tanzania officially launched the Universal Health Insurance Act in January 2026, marking a critical milestone toward Universal Health Coverage (UHC). Achieving UHC requires a fit-for-purpose primary care workforce. Family physicians, trained in the 5Cs of primary care — first-contact access, continuity, comprehensiveness, coordination, and person-centred care — are uniquely positioned to address fragmented care, rising non-communicable disease burden, and gaps in care coordination across Tanzania's tiered health system
Objectives: To describe the design and outcomes of the first Family Medicine Symposium in Tanzania and reflect on the informal multinational academic collaboration that drove it, as a model for advancing family medicine integration into national primary health care systems
Methods: A deliberative dialogue approach was employed at the first Family Medicine Symposium, Morogoro, January 28–29, 2026. Using the 5Cs framework, 51 Tanzanian and international stakeholders — including policymakers, clinicians, and academics from 11 countries — engaged in facilitated small-group discussions, expert presentations, and plenary synthesis to examine how family medicine can strengthen Tanzania's health system
Results: Participants identified health system gaps that family physicians are equipped to address: fragmented care coordination and referral pathways, delayed diagnosis, and rising multimorbidity from non-communicable diseases. Family physicians are currently trained at Aga Khan University; MUHAS and KCMC are developing MMed programmes. All respondents (100%) agreed the symposium strengthened their understanding of the 5Cs and its relevance to UHC. Absence of a defined scheme of service remains the key barrier to full deployment
Conclusion: Scaling up family medicine training, anchored in the 5Cs, will strengthen primary health care and advance UHC in Tanzania. Urgent action is needed to establish a formal scheme of service, enabling trained family physicians to practise to their full potential within the public health system
Perceptions of Clients on the Performance of Hospital Quality Improvement Teams at Arusha Lutheran Medical Centre
Maiko Malecela · Health Secretary (Intern) · Arusha Lutheran Medical Centre
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Quality improvement in healthcare has been recognized as a crucial strategy for enhancing patient outcomes and operational efficiency. In Tanzania, there is a wide range of quality improvement initiatives that aim at strengthening the country's health systems; however, some gaps still exist in their implementation
Objectives: This study aimed at assessing clients’ perceptions on the performance of hospital Quality Improvement Teams at Arusha Lutheran Medical Centre in Arusha region
Methods: The study adopted a cross-sectional design, recruited 105 participants, and employed both qualitative and quantitative approaches. Participants were selected using purposive and convenience sampling techniques, and data were collected using questionnaire and interview methods. Quantitative analysis was utilized in SPSS version 20, while qualitative data were analyzed thematically
Results: The level of awareness on the roles and functions of the Quality Improvement Teams was generally low among study participants; 79.2% (n=80) of respondents were not familiar with the roles and functions of Quality Improvement Team, indicating a gap in awareness. Additionally, there were unfavorable perceptions among clients on the effectiveness of the Quality Improvement Team; 64.3% (n=65) of respondents showed dissatisfaction with the quality improvement initiatives and reported low commitment of the Quality Improvement Teams. Several challenges affected the effectiveness of the Quality Improvement Teams: 62.3% (n=63) of respondents reported financial constraints, 53.3% (n=54) reported inadequate numbers of trained personnel, and 58.4% (n=59) indicated low client engagement and inconsistent support from the hospital administration
Conclusion: As unfavorable perceptions were revealed by patients on the performance of the Quality Improvement Teams, emphasis should be placed on raising awareness regarding the roles and functions of the Quality Improvement Teams, improving resource mobilization, and enhancing effective operation of quality improvement initiatives at the facility in order to improve the quality of healthcare services provided
Factors Influencing Medical Officer Interns’ Decision to Discontinue Clinical Practice after Internship in Dar Es Salaam, Tanzania
Nathaniel Jones · Resident · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: The transition from internship to independent clinical practice is a pivotal yet vulnerable phase in physicians’ careers. Emerging evidence suggests that many interns consider discontinuing clinical practice upon completion of internship, raising urgent concerns about early-career workforce retention, particularly in resource-constrained health systems
Objectives: To determine factors associated with medical officer interns’ decisions to discontinue clinical practice after internship in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Methods: A cross-sectional analytical census was conducted among medical officer interns in Dar es Salaam (n = 334). Data were collected using a structured questionnaire assessing socio-demographic, work environment, and psychosocial factors, including burnout, mentorship, effort–reward balance, psychological well-being, and career decision self-efficacy. Reliability was assessed using Cronbach’s alpha. Bivariate associations were tested using chi-square, and variables with p < 0.20 were included in multivariable logistic regression
Results: Nearly half (49.1%) reported intention to discontinue clinical practice. Participants were predominantly male (61.7%), aged 25–27 years (41.3%), and based in public hospitals (88.6%). Workload burden was high, with 48.2% reporting >80 hours weekly and frequent night shifts. Burnout (χ² = 8.371, p = 0.015) and reward (χ² = 7.334, p = 0.026) were significantly associated with discontinuation intention. Multivariable analysis showed no independent predictors; however, lower burnout (AOR 0.45, 95% CI: 0.17–1.17) demonstrated a suggestive protective association
Conclusion: A substantial proportion of interns consider discontinuing clinical practice, reflecting a multifactorial retention challenge. Health systems should implement Intern Workload Regulation Policy (IWRP), Structured Reward Optimization Model (SROM), National Internship Mentorship Framework (NIMF), Career Exposure Diversification Program (CEDP), Interns’ Voice and Feedback Integration System (IVFIS), Retention Monitoring Dashboard (RMD), and psychosocial support. Longitudinal studies are needed
Benefits, challenges, and the way Forward: Medical Specialists’ Perception on Family Physician Integration in Healthcare system in Tanzania
Juvenalius Anselm · Doctor · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Family medicine provides comprehensive care by integrating biomedical, behavioral, and social sciences. While established in some sub-Saharan countries, it remains new and unintegrated in Tanzania’s public health system. Its integration is vital for healthcare reform to enhance patient care and system quality. This study aimed to understand the perceptions of medical specialists regarding the integration of family physicians into the Tanzanian healthcare system
Objectives: The objectives of this study are to explore and establish the opinions of medical specialists on the integration of family physicians into the healthcare system, specifically to identify their perspectives on the associated benefits, examine the perceived challenges, and explore their recommendations for effective integration
Methods: A descriptive qualitative study was conducted through in-depth interviews with ten medical specialists from Aga Khan Hospital and Muhimbili National Hospital in Dar es Salaam. Thematic analysis was employed in this study
Results: Integration of family physicians into the healthcare system was widely supported, with benefits including holistic and comprehensive primary care, prevention, cost-effectiveness, early disease detection, and leadership. Challenges include limited awareness, lack of a defined scheme of service, and few training institutions. Recommendations include increasing awareness and advocacy, establishing structured scheme of service, expanding training institution, engaging the Ministry of Health, and strategic placement at district hospitals and the use of telemedicine to maximize their impact
Conclusion: Most specialists had a positive perception of integrating Family Physicians (FPs) into Tanzania’s healthcare system, recognizing it as a transformative opportunity to strengthen primary care, patient care and overall system quality. However, challenges such as limited awareness, the absence of a structured scheme of service, and limited training institutions hinder full recognition and deployment. Overcoming these barriers through advocacy, policy reforms, and expanded training programs is crucial for successful integration
Management of Clinical Alarms for Patients’ Safety
Salum Omary · Nurse · AKH
Co-authors: Aika Mongi
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Clinical alarms alert healthcare workers to physiological deterioration of patients, thereby supporting timely intervention. The Joint Commission International Accreditation (JCIA) reported 98 alarm events that included 80 deaths, 13 loss of function, and 5 extended hospital stays among patients admitted to the hospital. Clinical alarms were frequently ignored or improperly individualized according to patient condition. Delayed alarm response increased the risk of missed physiological deterioration
Objectives: A) Assess current clinical alarm management practices, staff knowledge, and attitudes across hospital units. B) Identify key factors contributing to alarm fatigue, including improper alarm settings, muted alarms, delayed responses, and compliance. C) Implement structured alarm management strategies—staff training, policy development, and equipment maintenance—to strengthen patient safety
Methods: This project was conducted at Aga Khan Hospital Dar es Salaam across critical care units (Emergency Room, Intensive Care Unit, Cath lab) as well as Paediatric, Maternity, Medical, Surgical, and Operating Theatre units. Questionnaires were administered that assessed knowledge, attitudes and practices of clinical alarms among the nursing staff. The clinical alarms observation tool was also used to assess the nurses during their shift
Results: 28% of staff responded to clinical alarm notifications, while 72% did not respond promptly to clinical alarm notification. Lack of knowledge, equipment failures, clinical device not audible, alarms not actionable, inadequate training and alarm fatigue hindered nurses to respond promptly to alarms
Conclusion: The clinical alarm project identified that alarm fatigue, improper alarm settings, and inadequate staff training weaken patient safety. Applying clinical alarm policy, continuous staff education, and device maintenance are essential for patient quality practices and achieving safe outcomes
The Pattern of Admission, Clinical Characteristics, and Outcomes among Patients Admitted to the Intensive Care Unit of a Tertiary Hospital in Tanzania: A 5-Year Retrospective Review
Nadeem Kassam · Doctor · AKH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Despite the implementation of complex interventions, ICU mortality remains high and more so in developing countries. The demand for critical care in Sub-Saharan Africa is more than ever before as the region experiences a double burden of rising rates of non-communicable diseases (NCD) in the background battle of combating infectious diseases. Limited studies in Tanzania have reported varying factors associated with markedly high rates of ICU mortality. Investigating the burden of ICU care remains crucial in providing insights into the effectiveness and challenges of critical care delivery
Objectives: To determine the burden of ICU mortality and identify factors associated with mortality among critically ill patients in Tanzania
Methods: A single-center retrospective study that reviewed records of all medically admitted patients admitted to the ICU of the Aga Khan Hospital, Dar-es-Salaam, from 1st October 2018 to 30th April 2023. To define the population in the study, we used descriptive statistics. Patients' outcomes were categorized based on ICU survival. Binary logistic regression was run (at 95% CI and p-value < 0.05) to identify the determinants for ICU mortality
Results: Medical records of 717 patients were reviewed. The cohort was male (n=472, 65.8%) and African predominant (n=471, 65.7%) with a median age of 58 years (IQR 45.0-71.0). 17.9% of patients did not survive. The highest mortality was noted amongst patients with septic shock (29.3%). The lowest survival was noted amongst patients requiring three organ support (n=12, 2.1%). Advanced age (OR 1.02,CI 1.00-1.04), having more than three underlying comorbidities (OR 2.50,CI 1.96-6.60), use of inotropic support (OR 3.58,CI 1.89-6.80) and mechanical ventilation (OR 9.11,CI 4.72-18.11) showed association with increased risk for mortality in ICU
Conclusion: The study indicated a much lower ICU mortality rate compared to similar studies conducted in other parts of Sub-Saharan Africa. Advanced age, underlying multiple comorbidities and organ support were associated with ICU mortality. Large multi-center studies are needed to highlight the true burden of critical care illness in Tanzania
Theme 4: Digital Health and Innovation for Quality Improvement
11:45–13:00 · Kivukoni 1 · Chair: Dr. Syed Hassan · Rapporteur: Dr. Ronak Mulji · Judges: Dr. Helga Mutasingwa & Dr. Syed Hassan
Improving Mothers’ Knowledge on Obstetric Violence Using a Contextualized mHealth Integrated System (VOICE-TZ): Evidence from a Quasi-experimental study in the Central Zone of Tanzania
Theresia Masoi · PhD Student in Midwifery · MUHAS
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Obstetric violence (OV) is an acknowledged human right violation during pregnancy, childbirth and after childbirth. Inadequate knowledge of obstetric violence limits women's ability to identify and report violence. Mobile health (mHealth) is a potential approach to health knowledge improvement, especially in resource constrained settings
Objectives: This study aimed at testing the effectiveness of the contextualized mHealth integrated system in enhancing knowledge of obstetric violence in central zone
Methods: A non-randomized controlled trial was conducted among 339 pregnant women (170 in the intervention group and 169 in the control group). Participants were followed from 32 weeks of pregnancy until six weeks postpartum. Women in the intervention group were enrolled in a contextualized mHealth integrated system (VOICE-TZ), which delivered health education messages on obstetric violence typologies twice weekly. The control group received routine antenatal care (ANC) health education provided at the local health facilities. SPSS software was used for both descriptive and inferential statistics
Results: At end line, knowledge levels increased in both groups, with a substantially higher proportion observed in the intervention group (82.9%) compared to the control group (56.2%). Linear mixed model analysis showed a statistically significant group by time interaction (β = 0.400; p = 0.005), indicating that the mHealth intervention resulted in a significantly greater improvement in knowledge compared to routine health education
Conclusion: The mHealth intervention was positively associated with improving knowledge of obstetric violence compared to routine care, highlighting the benefit of delivering contextualized educational content integrated within a digital platform. mHealth systems tailored to local contexts can be an effective, scalable strategy for enhancing maternal health education in resource constrained settings
The Kikohozi Classifier Project: Harnessing the Power of Cough Sounds for AI-Powered Diagnosis of Multiple Respiratory Diseases in Low-Income Africa
Kahabi Isangula · Researcher · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Respiratory diseases, including tuberculosis (TB), asthma, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), remain major health challenges in Tanzania, where diagnostic capacity is limited. Patients often face barriers accessing specialized facilities, contributing to delayed diagnosis and treatment. Advances in artificial intelligence (AI) offer new opportunities for scalable, non-invasive diagnostics
Objectives: To develop the Kikohozi Classifier, an AI-powered diagnostic tool capable of identifying TB, asthma, and COPD from cough sounds in primary healthcare and community settings across semi-urban and rural Tanzania
Methods: This study is establishing the Kikohozi-DB, Africa’s first large-scale cough sound database, by recruiting 30,000 participants and linking cough recordings to verified clinical diagnoses. Data are currently collected in healthcare facilities in collaboration with the National TB and Leprosy Program as well as in community settings. Using advanced AI/ML methods, cough audio is being pre-processed, relevant features extracted, and diagnostic models (Kikohozi-AI) trained and validated for real-world application across diverse devices and demographics
Results: Since the inception of data collection, we have recorded 3,800 cough sounds from 760 participants diagnosed with tuberculosis (56%), asthma (35%), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) (8%), and multiple respiratory conditions (1%). Preliminary analyses indicate promising potential in using cough sound characteristics to differentiate between major respiratory conditions. By the time of the conference, we anticipate presenting more concrete data and preliminary performance metrics
Conclusion: Tanzanian research capacity in AI/ML. Training programs, workshops, and local infrastructure are being conducted to ensure sustainability and foster a long-term AI-driven healthcare ecosystem
Promoting Exclusive Breastfeeding through Online Community-Based Support Groups in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Cynthia Masuka · Nutritionist · JJ Breastfeeding Organization
Co-authors: Idda Katigula, Dr. Mariam Noorani
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Optimal breastfeeding underpins maternal and infant nutrition, yet urban Tanzania's exclusive breastfeeding (EBF) rates fall short of global targets. Barriers include limited skilled lactation support, early maternal workforce return, sociocultural norms, and health system gaps. These drive suboptimal feeding practices, impairing infant nutrition, growth, and survival, underscoring the need for innovative, accessible peer support interventions
Objectives: This project aimed to strengthen maternal and infant nutrition by improving breastfeeding knowledge and practices through community-based peer breastfeeding support groups in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Methods: JJ Breastfeeding Organization (JJBO), partnering with Aga Khan Hospital and local authorities, targeted two municipal councils. Twenty community champions from public facilities received 18-hour training on lactation physiology, feeding guidelines, challenge management, maternal nutrition, counselling, and referrals. They launched facility-linked WhatsApp groups, enrolling pregnant/breastfeeding mothers. Mentorship, supervision, and facility engagement strengthened implementation. Monitoring assessed reach, participation, EBF rates, and support continuity
Results: Twenty champions from 20 facilities were trained, forming 20 groups. 955 mothers enrolled and engaged in peer education/counselling. Post-8-month intervention, EBF reached 72.88%, exceeding Dar es Salaam's 60.5% average. Health teams and staff received the intervention well. Limited smartphone access posed challenges, but facility-based education and direct engagement boosted enrolment/participation, demonstrating feasibility and effectiveness in urban settings despite digital divides
Conclusion: Champion-led digital peer groups effectively elevate EBF, integral with facilities for sustainability. Lessons: Hybrid online-facility models overcome access barriers; scale via government systems with refresher training ensures quality, advancing national nutrition targets
Role of Preventive Maintenance in Enhancing Patient Safety and Healthcare Quality
Godfred Nzumbi · Biomedical Technician · AKHST
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Biomedical equipment plays a critical role in diagnosis, treatment, and monitoring of patients in healthcare facilities, making equipment reliability essential for quality healthcare delivery and patient safety
Objectives: This quality improvement review assessed the role of preventive maintenance in reducing equipment downtime, improving operational efficiency, and supporting continuity of clinical services within a hospital setting
Methods: Data was reviewed from preventive maintenance schedules, maintenance records, service reports, and equipment performance logs managed by the biomedical engineering department
Results: The review identified that ineffective maintenance practices, delayed servicing, aging equipment, and inadequate maintenance tracking contributed to unexpected equipment failures, interruption of clinical workflows, delayed patient management, and increased dependency on backup systems or external referrals. Implementation of structured preventive maintenance schedules, routine equipment performance verification, improved documentation, and timely technical response contributed to increased equipment reliability, reduced downtime incidents, and improved coordination between clinical users and biomedical engineering personnel. In addition, preventive maintenance supported compliance with healthcare quality and patient safety standards by ensuring that medical equipment remained functional, safe, and available for patient care.
Conclusion: The findings demonstrate that strengthening preventive maintenance programs is an important strategy for improving healthcare quality, enhancing patient safety, and supporting efficient hospital operations. Healthcare facilities are encouraged to invest in effective biomedical equipment maintenance systems, digital maintenance tracking tools, and continuous technical capacity development to ensure sustainable and reliable healthcare service delivery.
Transforming Breast Imaging in Tanzania: From General Radiology to Sub-specialized Breast Imaging at Muhimbili National Hospital
Irene Mhalu · Doctor · MNH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Breast cancer remains the leading cancer among women globally and continues to pose a major health burden in Tanzania, where many patients present with advanced disease due to delayed diagnosis, limited screening services, and restricted access to specialized imaging services. Increasing referrals of female, male, and pediatric patients with breast-related conditions have further highlighted the need for dedicated breast imaging services within tertiary healthcare settings
Objectives: To document the transition of breast imaging services at Muhimbili National Hospital from general radiology practice into a sub-specialized breast imaging unit, while highlighting current services, institutional experience, existing challenges, and future directions
Methods: This concept note is based on institutional experience and service development within the Women Imaging Unit at MNH through review of clinical workflow, imaging services, interventional procedures, training activities, and multidisciplinary breast cancer care practices
Results: Since establishment of the subspecialized unit in 2023, breast imaging services at Muhimbili National Hospital have transitioned from general radiology using conventional 2D mammography to a sub-specialized breast imaging unit with 3D mammography/tomosynthesis and stereotactic biopsy capability. Current services for adult female, male, and pediatric patients include mammography, breast ultrasound, breast MRI, image-guided procedures, and breast imaging clinic consultations. The unit has performed 434 screening mammograms, 1,697 diagnostic mammograms, and 2,843 breast ultrasounds, while performing an average of five ultrasound-guided biopsies weekly
Conclusion: The establishment of sub-specialized breast imaging services at MNH demonstrates the feasibility and importance of dedicated breast imaging services in strengthening multidisciplinary breast cancer care within resource-limited settings
Recommendation: Continued investment in breast imaging infrastructure, permanent image storage systems, advanced interventional services, workforce development, and multidisciplinary breast cancer care pathways is recommended to strengthen specialized breast imaging services in Tanzania
Transforming Antimicrobial Stewardship through Electronic Health Record Integration: Post-Implementation Experience from Aga Khan Hospital, Dar es Salaam
Charity Chiwambo · Pharmacist · AKH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) continues to pose a major threat to patient safety and healthcare systems globally. At Aga Khan Hospital, Dar es Salaam, a structured Antimicrobial Stewardship Programme (ASP) established in 2017 was strengthened through the implementation of a fully integrated Electronic Health Record (EHR) system (MEDITECH) in 2025. The system was designed to embed stewardship principles into routine clinical workflows to overcome limitations of manual processes, delayed feedback, and fragmented data systems
Objectives: The objective was to evaluate the impact of EHR implementation on antimicrobial stewardship performance by comparing quarterly Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) before and after system deployment
Methods: A retrospective post-implementation evaluation was conducted comparing ASP KPIs from the last pre-implementation quarter (Q3 2025) and the first post-implementation quarter (Q1 2026). Indicators assessed included antimicrobial prescribing compliance, culture collection prior to initiation of restricted antibiotics, surgical prophylaxis adherence, and appropriate use of restricted antimicrobials. Data were extracted from EHR dashboards, pharmacy records, and microbiology reports. Stakeholder feedback from clinicians, pharmacists, and microbiology teams supplemented quantitative analysis
Results: Following EHR implementation, significant improvements were observed in antimicrobial stewardship performance. Rational antimicrobial use increased to 89.4%, supported by real-time clinical decision support and automated prescribing alerts. Compliance with culture collection prior to restricted antimicrobial initiation improved to 91%, while adherence to appropriate surgical prophylaxis timing reached 99%. Integration of antibiotic timeout alerts, mandatory indication documentation, and automated renewal controls reduced prolonged and inappropriate antimicrobial exposure
Conclusion: Post-implementation findings demonstrate that integration of ASP within an EHR significantly strengthens antimicrobial governance, improves prescribing quality, and enhances patient safety. Digital workflows enabled sustained behavioural change, improved accountability, and real-time clinical decision support. This model provides a scalable framework for digital transformation of antimicrobial stewardship in similar healthcare settings
Theme 5: Quality and Continuity of Care Across the Life Course
11:45–13:00 · Kivukoni 2 · Chair: Dr. Rahma Bakari · Rapporteur: Dr. Murtaza Lamuwalla · Judges: Prof. Philip Adebayo & Dr. Rahma Bakari
Extending Hospital Care into the Community: A Home-Based Model Improving Access, Continuity, and Patient Safety in Dar es salaam, Tanzania
Said Shukuru · Nurse · AKH
Co-authors: Aika Mongi
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Continuity of care following hospital discharge remains a challenge for patients requiring ongoing clinical support, particularly in resource-limited settings. Barriers such as mobility, cost, and access to facility-based follow-up contribute to care gaps. The H2H2H (Hospital-to-Home-to-Hospital) program was designed to extend healthcare delivery into patients’ homes, improving accessibility, continuity, and patient-centred outcomes through structured home-based services
Objectives: To evaluate the effectiveness of a home-based care model in improving access, service utilization, patient safety, and continuity of care using defined quality indicators
Methods: A retrospective quality improvement review of H2H2H program data for 2025 was conducted using a KPI dashboard. Indicators included patient enrolment, service utilization, adverse events, readmission rates, and patient satisfaction. Patients discharged from hospital were assessed for eligibility and enrolled into structured home-based care services, including nursing care, physiotherapy, and clinical monitoring
Results: A total of 1,049 patients were enrolled into the program from 1,508 screened, delivering a high volume of home-based care activities, predominantly nursing services. The program maintained low adverse events (1 reported) and consistently low readmission rates (≤3 monthly), indicating safe care delivery. Service utilization remained stable despite fluctuations across specific service lines. Patient satisfaction response rates improved over time, exceeding 95% in later periods, although overall participation remained variable
Conclusion: Home-based care is a safe and feasible model for improving access and continuity. Strengthening patient engagement, expanding coverage, and improving feedback mechanisms can enhance uptake and equity in care delivery
Determinants of Self-Medication Practices for Cough and Cold among Caregivers of Children in Dar-es-Salaam-A Mixed Methods Study
Sheliza Thaver · Resident · Aga Khan University
Co-authors: Dr Nahida Walli, Dr Mariam Noorani, Dr Neelam Ismail, Dr Maria Bulimba
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Self-medication practice is recognized as a significant global public health concern. The inappropriate use of medicines often coupled with inadequate knowledge poses considerable risks to public health, particularly among the paediatric population. Caregivers frequently administer medications to children without professional medical advice for common illnesses such as cough and cold. Cough and cold medications lack strong evidence supporting their effectiveness and have been associated with adverse effects, especially in young children
Objectives: The primary objective was to explore the determinants of self-medication practices among caregivers for the treatment of cough and cold in children under five years. The secondary objectives were to determine the proportion of self-medication practices among caregivers, type of medication used and common sources of medicines for the treatment of cough and cold in children under five years
Methods: This was a mixed-methods study employing a concurrent embedded study design that incorporated an analytic cross-sectional and a descriptive qualitative design. The study population was caregivers of children under five years of age who presented with cough and cold to the outpatient departments of Aga Khan Health Service Clinics in Dar-es-Salaam, Tanzania
Results: The proportion of self-medication practices among caregivers of children for the treatment of cough and cold was 70.9%, cough syrup was the most common medicine used (51.4%) and the common source of obtaining medicines was from pharmacies (75.8%). From the quantitative study, the determinants of self-medication practices were older age of caregiver, being a mother and having two or more children in the household From the qualitative study, the determinants of self-medication practices were grouped into the following themes: Influence of health care workers and family, barriers within the health care system, perceived familiarity of illness and caregivers’ medical background that promote self-medication practices among caregivers of children with cough and cold
Conclusion: This study demonstrated a high prevalence of self-medication among caregivers for the treatment of cough and cold in children. While the use of safe home remedies like honey may be appropriate for cough and cold, the widespread use of over-the-counter medicines such as cough syrups raises concerns regarding their potential risks in children Addressing this issue requires a coordinated, multi-sectoral approach involving strengthening regulatory frameworks, enhancing community-based health education, promoting pharmacists to adopt evidence-based dispensing practices and establishing clear telemedicine protocols to ensure safer prescribing practices among health care professionals
From Culture to Cure: The Clinical Microbiology laboratory as the Engine of Evidence-Based Quality Care and Antibiotic Stewardship at a Tertiary Hospital in Tanzania
Jonathan Shogholo · Laboratory Scientist · AKH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) represents one of the most critical threats to global public health, disproportionately burdening low and middle-income countries (LMIC) including Tanzania. Hospital Antibiotic Stewardship Programs (ASPs) are internationally recognized as a primary strategy to optimize antimicrobial use, preserve antibiotic efficacy, and improve patient outcomes. Central to any effective ASP is the clinical microbiology laboratory, which provides microbiological intelligence, including culture and sensitivity data, cumulative antibiograms, and surveillance of Multidrug-Resistant Organisms (MDROs). That forms the evidentiary foundation for rational antibiotic prescribing
Objectives: To demonstrate the impact of a clinical microbiology laboratory on quality improvement and patient safety intervention on antimicrobial stewardship, prescribing practices, and clinical decision-making to improve patient outcomes and strengthen health systems
Methods: A retrospective analysis of laboratory-driven Antibiotic Stewardship Programs (ASP) interventions was conducted. Key focus areas included the implementation of automated diagnostic platforms, the standardization of selective reporting of susceptibility results, and the annual development of facility-specific cumulative antibiograms to guide empirical therapy protocols
Results: The integration of automated diagnostic equipment in clinical microbiology laboratory significantly improved patient safety by reducing the time to effective therapy specifically through the presence of automated pathogen identification and antibiotic susceptibility equipment (Vitek 2 Compact) that bridges the gap between diagnostic speed and clinical decision making. Furthermore, utilization of cumulative antibiogram data led to an improvement in the appropriateness of empirical prescriptions
Conclusion: The microbiology laboratory is not merely a service provider but a strategic partner in health system strengthening. The clinical microbiology laboratory functions as an indispensable pillar of the hospital’s Antibiotic Stewardship Program, generating actionable microbiological evidence that directly informs prescribing decisions, infection control practices, and AMR surveillance
Co-Created Clinical Practice Guidelines and Recurring In-House Training to Improve Healthy Survival at Birth in Urban Tanzania: A Stepped-Wedge Cluster-Randomised Trial (PartoMa Birth)
Natasha Housseine · Resident · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Birth-related mortality constitutes a global health burden, disproportionately affecting low- and lower-middle-income countries. Increasingly, these deaths are concentrated in urban areas with congested health facilities. Clinical practice guidelines are a central step in strengthening timely, evidence-based care. However, these are often developed with little regard for the contexts and conditions that shape their implementation
Objectives: To assess the effect on perinatal mortality of locally co-created, and thereby contextualised, clinical practice guidelines, which were implemented through quarterly three-hour in-house seminars in urban Tanzania
Methods: A stepped-wedge cluster-randomised trial was conducted in five governmental high-volume maternity units in Dar es Salaam. The units were randomised at facility level into three intervention sequences, with sequential roll-out at three-month intervals. Birth data were extracted from facility registries, with regular data quality assessments. Among women with singleton, inborn babies weighing ≥1000g, the primary composite outcome was the standardised risk of perinatal mortality, including stillbirths with foetal heart rate on admission and intrahospital 7-day neonatal mortality. The primary model adjusted for categorical calendar month to estimate a site-collapsed standardised before-versus-after contrast under the observed rollout. Also, two hospital-adjusted sensitivity analyses were conducted
Results: In total, 65,181 mother-baby pairs were included; 22,338 before and 42,843 after implementation. In the primary model without hospital adjustment, the standardised risk of perinatal mortality decreased from 21·25 to 11·05 per 1000 births (risk ratio (RR) 0·52, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0·34-0·79, p=0.002). Stillbirths with a positive foetal heart rate recorded on admission fell from 8·26 to 2·51 per 1000 births (RR 0·30, 95% CI 0·13-0·73, p=0·008), and 7-day intrahospital neonatal mortality fell from 13·71 to 8·56 per 1000 live births (RR 0·62, 95% CI 0·41-0.96, p=0·030). In the hospital-adjusted sensitivity analyses, stillbirth with a positive foetal heart rate recorded on admission remained reduced (RR 0·46, 95% CI 0·25–0·86 and RR 0·43, 95% CI 0·19–0·96), while neonatal mortality, composite perinatal mortality, and overall stillbirth appeared sensitive to differences in hospital composition
Conclusion: Birth-related perinatal mortality reduced following the introduction of co-created, contextualised clinical practice guidelines and related in-house training. Our results disclose the underexplored potential of co-creating context-specific clinical support tools that could integrate global evidence-based recommendations with local clinical realities, both within maternal and neonatal health care and potentially beyond
Factors Associated with Screen Time Use among Preschool Children in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Elisamia Ngowi · Doctor · Aga Khan University
Co-authors: Maria Bulimba, Mariam Noorani
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Excessive screen time in early childhood has been associated with poor sleep, overweight, and developmental delays. The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends no screen exposure for children under 2 years and a maximum of 1 hour per day for those aged 2–5 years. Despite these recommendations, many children exceed these limits. In Tanzania, no national or professional guidelines exist on screen use in young children. Understanding adherence to WHO recommendations and identifying factors contributing to excessive screen exposure is essential for developing targeted interventions to promote healthy behaviours
Objectives: The primary objective was to determine factors associated with screen time usage among preschool children in Dar es Salaam. The secondary objectives were; a) To determine the average daily amount of screen time among preschool children in Dar es Salaam and b) To determine compliance to WHO guidelines on screen time usage among preschool children in Dar es Salaam
Methods: We conducted an analytical cross-sectional study among 499 children aged 2–5 years and their caregivers from preschools in Ilala District, Dar es Salaam. Data were collected using structured caregiver questionnaires administered both online and in paper form. Descriptive statistics were used to summarize screen time exposure, and logistic regression identified factors associated with adherence to WHO guidelines (≤1 hour/day). The primary outcome was the proportion of children meeting WHO screen time recommendations
Results: Only 8.6% of children adhered to WHO guidelines, with a mean daily screen time of four hours. In unadjusted analyses, excessive screen time was associated with the presence of a television at home (COR 2.85; 95% CI 1.48–5.50; p = 0.001), caregiver perception that screens disrupt sleep (COR 0.49; 95% CI 0.24–0.97; p = 0.041), and being cared for primarily by biological mothers (COR 0.20; 95% CI 0.05–0.86; p = 0.017). None of these associations remained statistically significant after adjustment for potential confounders
Conclusion: The majority (91.4%) of preschool children in our study exceeded WHO screen time guidelines. While device availability and caregiver-related factors appear influential, adjusted analyses did not confirm independent associations. These findings highlight the need for national guidelines, caregiver education, and school-based programs to promote healthier screen use and reduce the risks of excessive screen exposure in early childhood
Exploring Challenges and Opportunities for Integrating Carbetocin into Maternal Healthcare in Tanzania: Perspectives from Healthcare Workers and Key Stakeholders
Euphraim Massanga · Resident · Aga Khan University
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) remains a leading cause of maternal mortality, particularly in low- and middle-income countries such as Tanzania. Effective uterotonic prophylaxis during the third stage of labor is essential. Although oxytocin is recommended as first-line therapy, its reliance on cold-chain storage limits effectiveness in some settings. Heat-stable carbetocin offers a potential alternative due to its prolonged action and temperature stability, but its integration into routine care requires addressing health system and policy factors
Objectives: To explore challenges and opportunities for integrating carbetocin into Tanzania’s maternal health system for PPH prevention and management
Methods: A descriptive qualitative study was conducted using purposive sampling. Participants included obstetricians, anesthesiologists, pharmacists, nurses, and midwives at Aga Khan Hospital Dar es Salaam, as well as policymakers from the Ministry of Health and representatives from a professional body. Data were collected through key informant interviews and focus group discussions. Thematic analysis was used to identify key implementation barriers and facilitators
Results: Five interrelated themes emerged. Carbetocin was perceived as clinically effective and operationally advantageous due to its sustained uterotonic effect and heat stability. However, integration was influenced by procurement challenges, supply chain limitations, and policy prioritization.
