Provider acceptance evolved from initial skepticism to confidence following positive clinical experience. Cost remained a major concern, although participants noted potential long-term savings through reduced PPH-related complications
Conclusion: Carbetocin is viewed as a promising intervention for improving PPH prevention in Tanzania. Addressing financial, policy, and implementation barriers, alongside strengthening local evidence, may support its integration into national maternal health strategies
Theme 6: Quality of Research and Teaching in Health Care
11:45–13:00 · Kivukoni 3 · Chair: Dr. Amri Kyaruzi · Rapporteur: Dr. Zeenat Juneja · Judges: Prof. Francis Furia & Dr. Amri Kyaruzi
Strengthening Evidence-Based Healthcare Through Research Capacity Building: Experience from Shree Hindu Mandal Research and Training, Dar es Salaam - Tanzania
Khadija Abdallah · Researcher · Shree Hindu Mandal Research and Training
Co-authors: Jackline Zambi, Magreth Angelus, Berthania Magesa, Kinari Swali
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Healthcare institutions generate large amounts of patient data during routine care, yet much of these data remain underutilized due to limited research and analytical capacity. Failure to use local data may result in overlooked health challenges and missed opportunities for evidence-based healthcare improvement. To address this gap, Shree Hindu Mandal Dar es Salaam established the Research and Training Unit (SHM R&T) in August 2023
Objectives: To describe the establishment and early contributions of the Shree Hindu Mandal Research and Training Unit in promoting research capacity and evidence-based healthcare in Tanzania
Methods: An institutional review of activities and outputs of SHM R&T since its establishment in August 2023 was conducted using records of research projects, collaborations, publications, and training initiatives
Results: Since its establishment, SHM R&T has coordinated multiple projects including multicenter clinical trial such (META Trial) and established collaborations with institutions including UCL, ISGlobal, UCD, Umea University, Nova Biomedical, and NIMR. Members affiliated with the Unit have contributed to over 100 peer-reviewed publications. The Unit has also trained over 500 individuals including medical students, interns, clinicians, nurses, and allied healthcare professionals. In addition, SHM R&T has strengthened staff capacity in research ethics, Good Clinical Practice, scientific writing, proposal development, and grant management. Analysis of routinely collected data and institutional case studies has also supported identification of important findings that may previously have been overlooked
Conclusion: SHM R&T demonstrates how institutional investment in research and training can transform routinely collected healthcare data into meaningful evidence for improving patient care and strengthening evidence-based healthcare in Tanzania. Similar institutional research platforms should be strengthened to enhance local evidence generation and support data-driven healthcare decision-making
Strengthening Quality Research and Teaching in Health University through Academic Libraries: Experiences from Muhimbili University of Health and Allied Sciences
Rehema Chande · Librarian · MUHAS
Co-authors: Neema Maluka
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Quality research and effective teaching are essential for strengthening health systems and advancing evidence-based healthcare education. Academic libraries support research productivity, information literacy, scholarly communication, and access to scientific information for clinical decision-making. However, many institutions in developing countries continue to face challenges in accessing adequate electronic resources and research support services
Objectives: To examine the contribution of the Muhimbili University of Health and Allied Sciences Library in improving research quality and teaching excellence between 2022 and 2025. Specifically, the study assessed the extent to which library research support services enhanced research productivity and teaching, and evaluated the impact of scholarly communication initiatives
Methods: A descriptive institutional review was conducted using library reports, repository statistics, bibliometric records, training reports, and plagiarism screening data collected between 2022 and 2025. In addition, eight purposively selected key informants were interviewed
Results: During the review period, the library strengthened research and teaching through multiple support services. Journal selection guidance supported 3,480 users, literature searching assistance reached 2,964 users, and referencing and citation support assisted 2,736 users. A total of 186 training sessions engaged 4,720 participants in information literacy, database searching, systematic reviews, reference management, research ethics, and academic writing. Library research support contributed to over 630 researchers, while MUHAS-affiliated publications received more than 11,843 citations globally. The institutional repository expanded from 1,840 items in 2022 to 3,207 items in 2025, while annual research publications increased from 690 to 851, improving institutional visibility and scholarly communication. To strengthen academic integrity, 1,548 Turnitin plagiarism checks were conducted, and 1,692 users received support with UpToDate registration and verification, thereby promoting evidence-based practice. Increased utilization of library services was associated with improved research productivity, stronger compliance with ethical publishing standards, and enhanced teaching quality
Conclusion: Academic libraries are strategic contributors to research quality, scholarly communication, academic integrity, and evidence-based teaching. Sustained investment in digital library infrastructure and research support services is essential for advancing healthcare education and research excellence in Tanzania and other resource-limited settings. In addition, university management and policymakers should recognize academic libraries as strategic partners in healthcare education and research
Improving Emergency Care in Tanzania: Scaling Capacity and Improving systems through a Public–Private Partnership Model
Hussein Manji · Doctor · Aga Khan Hospital
Co-authors: Aleksandra Mizerska, Elishah Premji, Sisawo Konteh
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Emergency care gaps in Tanzania significantly contribute to preventable mortality from time-sensitive conditions. Baseline assessments in 2022 identified deficiencies in workforce capacity, systems, and infrastructure. Fewer than 30% of frontline providers had formal emergency training. Triage delays of 20–30 minutes for high-acuity patients were common. Over 40% of essential equipment was unavailable or non-functional. Prehospital care remained informal, with limited training and underdeveloped response systems. Low clinician confidence and poor simulation exposure worsened outcomes, contributing to delays, inefficiencies, and burnout. IMECT addresses these gaps through training, infrastructure investment, and system strengthening for sustainable scale across Tanzania healthcare systems nationwide
Objectives: The IMECT project strengthened emergency care in Tanzania by training 2,336 providers, exceeding its 1,500 target, improving clinical systems, and deploying essential equipment across seven facilities. It established a national training center, trained 160 instructors, supported first responders, and implemented a scalable public–private partnership model with measurable improvements in care quality, efficiency, and sustainability
Methods: IMECT used a structured, multi-component approach combining needs assessments, training, infrastructure, and system strengthening. Over 250 sessions delivered blended learning with simulation and bedside mentorship by international and local experts, improving knowledge scores. A training-of-trainers model produced 160 instructors, supported by a national simulation center. Continuous supervision, equipment deployment, and first responder training strengthened care delivery. Strong stakeholder engagement and monitoring ensured effective, sustainable implementation across supported facilities
Results: The IMECT project achieved measurable improvements in workforce capacity, clinical quality, and operations. A total of 2,336 providers were trained, with improved knowledge, skills, and confidence through simulation and mentorship. Standardized triage systems doubled the proportion of patients receiving quality care, alongside better guideline adherence. Facilities reported reduced emergency deaths and referrals, improved patient flow, and faster critical interventions. High participant satisfaction and strengthened infrastructure across seven facilities highlight IMECT as a scalable, cost-effective model
Conclusion: The IMECT project achieved strong stakeholder support and system-level impact, improving emergency care quality, clinical outcomes, and workforce confidence. Nearly 2,300 trained providers drove sustained practice change. Strengthened triage systems, monitoring frameworks, and infrastructure enhanced efficiency across facilities. The model is scalable, with national relevance and potential for replication in similar resource-limited settings
Effect of Learning Infrastructures on Nursing Students’ Academic Performance at Dodoma Institute of Health and Allied Sciences: An Outcome Evaluation of the Continuous Quality Improvement Guideline
Mwahija Amrani · Nurse · Dodoma Regional Referral Hospital
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Learning infrastructures are critical determinants of academic performance in health training institutions, particularly in nursing education where theoretical instruction must be supported by practical learning environments. In Tanzania, the implementation of the Continuous Quality Improvement (CQI) Guideline in health training institutions aimed to strengthen teaching and learning quality through improved infrastructures and continuous monitoring. Despite these efforts, limited empirical evidence exists regarding the contribution of learning infrastructures to nursing students’ academic performance. This study assessed the effect of learning infrastructures on academic performance among nursing students at Dodoma Institute of Health and Allied Sciences (DIHAS)
Objectives: The study aimed to evaluate the effect of learning infrastructures on nursing students’ academic performance at DIHAS. Specifically, the study assessed the contribution of ICT facilities, skills laboratories, library resources, accommodation, and clinical placement sites to students’ academic outcomes before and after CQI implementation
Methods: A quasi-experimental study design was employed to compare pre-CQI (2019) and post-CQI (2024) cohorts. The study involved 172 nursing students selected from institutional databases. Data were collected using structured questionnaires administered both physically and online. Descriptive statistics and multiple linear regression with robust standard errors were performed using STATA software to determine the association between learning infrastructures and academic performance measured by GPA. Ethical clearance was obtained from Mzumbe University, and permission to conduct the study was granted by DIHAS
Results: The study found significant improvements in learning infrastructures following CQI implementation. Access to ICT facilities, skills laboratories, and library resources increased substantially in the post-CQI period. Multiple linear regression analysis revealed that ICT facilities (β = 0.42, p < 0.05), skills laboratories (β = 0.31, p < 0.05), and library resources (β = 0.27, p < 0.05) significantly improved academic performance. Adequate classrooms, computer availability, and diverse clinical exposure were also positively associated with higher GPA scores. The regression model was statistically significant (F = 59.68, p < 0.001), explaining 78.1% of the variance in academic performance
Conclusion: Learning infrastructures have a significant positive effect on nursing students’ academic performance at DIHAS. The implementation of the CQI Guideline contributed to improved learning environments, particularly through enhanced ICT facilities, practical skills laboratories, and library resources. Strengthening educational infrastructures is therefore essential for improving nursing education quality and producing competent healthcare. The study recommends sustained investment in ICT facilities, skills laboratories, and library resources within health training institutions. Policymakers and institutional leaders should strengthen implementation of CQI guidelines to ensure continuous improvement of learning environments
Perception and Challenges of using Artificial Intelligence in Post Graduate Medical Education in Dar es salaam, Tanzania
Dr. Nahida Walli · Paediatrician · Aga Khan University
Co-authors: Mariam Noorani, Aliakbar Zehri, Hajaj Salum, Hanifa Mbithe, Daudi Gidion, Elisamia Ngowi
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Artificial intelligence (AI) can be defined as a machine with intelligent behaviour such as perception, reasoning, learning, and the ability to perform human tasks. AI is increasingly being integrated into healthcare and medical education, offering opportunities to improve learning efficiency and educational delivery. However, concerns remain regarding its ethical use, reliability, and impact on clinical reasoning, particularly in low- and middle-income countries where evidence is limited. This study will inform and create a good foundation to influence future anticipated changes in medical education, research and overall curriculum development
Objectives: To explore perceptions and challenges related to the use of AI in postgraduate medical education among residents and faculty members in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Methods: This descriptive qualitative study was conducted across three universities providing postgraduate medical education in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania. Participants included postgraduate medical residents and faculty members involved in postgraduate training. Data were collected through four Focus Group Discussions (FGDs) with residents and fifteen Key Informant Interviews (KIIs) with faculty members. Interviews were audio-recorded, transcribed verbatim, and analyzed using content analysis with MAXQDA version 24
Results: A total of 27 residents and 13 faculty members participated. Five major themes emerged: (1) AI as a supportive learning tool, (2) concerns regarding overreliance and reduced clinical reasoning, (3) concerns about the accuracy and contextual relevance of AI-generated information, (4) barriers to effective AI use within the local context, and (5) the need for structured institutional support
Conclusion: AI is perceived as a valuable supportive tool in postgraduate medical education in Tanzania. However, concerns regarding overreliance, reliability of information, contextual limitations, and inadequate institutional guidance remain significant barriers. Structured training, institutional policies, and improved access to reliable AI platforms are essential for responsible integration of AI into medical education
Role of Preimplantation Genetic Testing in Enhancing IVF Outcomes: A Focus on Implantation and Pregnancy Rates
Muralidhar Babu · Embryologist · Nulife Advanced Fertility Centre
Co-authors: Dr. Madhav Hirani, Dr. Sheila Karim Ramadhani
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Preimplantation Genetic Testing (PGT) has evolved as an important step in in vitro fertilization (IVF) technology, and this technique has the potential to identify chromosomally normal embryos prior to transfer. Aneuploidy is one of the major factors contributing to implantation failure and miscarriages, especially in patients with advanced maternal age and history of Implantation failure. This technique has the potential to enhance implantation and clinical pregnancy rates and decrease the miscarriage rate. Emerging clinical data from Tanzania and among international patients undergoing treatment in this setting demonstrate improved implantation and pregnancy outcomes with PGT, supporting its effectiveness in this population
Objectives: To assess the impact of Preimplantation Genetic Testing (PGT) on implantation rate and clinical pregnancy outcomes in IVF cycles in a Tanzanian clinical setting, including both local and cross-border patient populations
Methods: This retrospective study was conducted at Nulife Advanced Fertility Center (Air Med Healthcare Company Ltd.), Tanzania. IVF cycles with Preimplantation Genetic Testing (PGT) were included. Controlled ovarian stimulation was followed by oocyte retrieval and ICSI fertilization. Embryos were cultured to blastocyst stage, biopsied on Day 5/6, and analyzed by Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS). Only euploid embryos were thawed and transferred in FET cycles. Outcomes assessed were implantation and clinical pregnancy rates among local and international patients
Results: A total of 242 IVF cycles with PGT were analyzed. 680 embryos were biopsied, of which 55% (374) were euploid. 354 euploid embryos were subsequently thawed and transferred in frozen embryo transfer (FET) cycles. The overall implantation rate and clinical pregnancy rate were 67%. Both local and international patients achieved favorable outcomes, with a low early pregnancy loss rate of 6%, demonstrating the effectiveness of PGT-guided embryo selection in improving IVF success
Conclusion: Preimplantation Genetic Testing (PGT) greatly improved implantation and pregnancy outcomes at our center for both local and international patients. We recommend PGT-A as part of routine IVF care, especially for women with advanced maternal age or previous implantation failures, to enhance success rates and reduce the risk of miscarriage
Theme 1: Quality Improvement and Patient Safety in Clinical Practice
10:30–11:45 · Kivukoni 1 · Chair: Aminieli Usiri · Rapporteur: Dr. Daisy Mhando · Judges: Prof. Daudi Simba & Aminieli Usiri
Effectiveness of Innovated Eye Shield for phototherapy compared to improvised materials and imported eye shield: An Experimental Study
Consolata Matee · Doctor · MUHAS
Risk Factors and Clinical Outcomes in Hospitalized Patients with Multi Drug Resistant Organism (MDRO) Infections admitted in a Tertiary Hospital in Tanzania
Samina Somji · Doctor · AKU/AKH
Enhancing Quality Improvement in IVF Practice Through Effective Quality Control in the Embryology Laboratory

Effectiveness of a structured handover checklist in the post anesthesia care unit on reducing information loss and complication rates at MNH
Shivangi Mandania · Resident · MUHAS
Improving Quality of Clinicians handover using the ISBAR tool in the Paediatric and Neonatal units at a Tertiary hospital in Tanzania

Exploration of the Impact of Reconstructive Surgery on the Quality of Life of Women with Burn Related Deformities in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study

Early Breastfeeding Challenges and Associated Risk Factors Among First-Time Mothers in Dar es Salaam & Pwani Regions of Tanzania

The Efficacy and Safety of Greater Occipital Nerve Block for Migraine in Pregnancy: A Systematic Review

Exploring quality of life in postoperative rheumatic Heart Disease Patients at Jakaya Kikwete Cardiac Institute

Understanding Barriers Associated with Delays in Diagnosis and Treatment Among Colorectal Cancer Patients Treated at a Tertiary-Level Care Hospital, Tanzania

Perceptions of Clients on the Performance of Hospital Quality Improvement Teams at Arusha Lutheran Medical Centre

Factors Influencing Medical Officer Interns' Decision to Discontinue Clinical Practice After Internship in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania

Benefits, Challenges, and the Way Forward: Medical Specialists' Perception on Family Physician Integration in Healthcare System in Tanzania

The Kikohozi Classifier Project: Harnessing the Power of Cough Sounds for AI-Powered Diagnosis of Multiple Respiratory Diseases in Low-Income Africa

Role of Preventive Maintenance in Enhancing Patient Safety and Health Care Quality

Transforming Breast Imaging in Tanzania: From General Radiology to Subspecialized Breast Imaging at Muhimbili National Hospital

Extending Hospital Care into the Community: A Home-Based Model Improving Access, Continuity, and Patient Safety in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania

Khadija Abdallah · Researcher · Shree Hindu Mandal Research and Training

Role of Preimplantation Genetic Testing in Enhancing IVF Outcomes: A Focus on Implantation and Pregnancy Rates
Muralidhar Babu · Embryologist · Nulife Advanced Fertility Centre
Poster Presentations
Tuberculous Aortitis Complicated by Aortic Root Pseudo aneurysm and Moderate Aortic Regurgitation in a Young Adult: A Case Report and Review of Management
Ally Hamza Mzige · Doctor · AKH
Co-authors: Nadeem Kassam, Mandela Makakala, Christopher Mwansasu
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Tuberculous aortitis is a rare but potentially life threatening manifestation of tuberculosis that may result in aneurysm or pseudoaneurysm formation, aortic valve dysfunction, and rupture. Early diagnosis is challenging because clinical manifestations are often nonspecific
Case Presentation: A 29-year-old man with pulmonary tuberculosis on the third month of anti-tuberculosis therapy presented with recurrent dizziness, palpitations, chest discomfort, and a syncopal episode. Initial laboratory investigations were largely unremarkable except for persistently elevated inflammatory markers (ESR 71.28 mm/hr). Echocardiography demonstrated dilatation of the aortic root and ascending aorta with moderate aortic regurgitation and preserved left ventricular systolic function. CT aortography and subsequent cardiac CT revealed inflammatory aortitis involving the ascending aorta and aortic arch, complicated by a large aortic root pseudoaneurysm arising from the non-coronary sinus measuring approximately 6 × 5.5 × 4 cm, encroaching on the adjacent left and right atria. Alternative causes including infectious and autoimmune conditions were considered, with Takayasu arteritis and connective tissue disorder–associated aortopathy remaining differential diagnoses
Management and Outcome: The patient was continued on anti-tuberculosis therapy and commenced on beta-blocker therapy for blood pressure and shear stress control. Following multidisciplinary discussions with local and international cardiothoracic teams, definitive surgical management with a Bentall procedure (aortic root replacement) was recommended because of the high risk of pseudoaneurysm enlargement, rupture, and progressive valvular dysfunction
Conclusion: This case highlights the importance of considering tuberculous aortitis in patients with tuberculosis who develop unexplained aortic pathology, particularly in endemic regions. Multimodality imaging played a crucial role in diagnosis, while early multidisciplinary involvement was essential for planning definitive surgical intervention. Prompt recognition and treatment may improve outcomes in this rare but life-threatening condition
Metaplastic Breast Carcinoma in Tanzania: A Rare Case Highlighting Diagnostic and Therapeutic Challenges
Bahati Kamwezi · Doctor · KCMC
Co-authors: Agnes L Mlawa, Tresphory Boniphace Kamushaga, Tulia M Hadi, Lusekelo W Mwalukambwe, Elias Edrick, Mirko Lombardi
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Metaplastic breast carcinoma (MBC) is a rare and aggressive subtype of breast cancer, accounting for less than 1% of cases. Diagnosis requires advanced pathology and immunohistochemistry, which are often unavailable in low-resource settings. In Tanzania, poverty and the absence of widespread oncologic centers further limit access to timely diagnosis and modern treatment modalities. We present a case of metaplastic breast carcinoma in a young woman in Tanzania, emphasizing the rarity of the disease, the diagnostic difficulties, and the therapeutic limitations imposed by socioeconomic and infrastructural constraints
Case Presentation: A 38-year-old woman presented with a left breast lump and bloody nipple discharge persisting for ten months. An initial excision at a local hospital was performed without histological examination or documentation. Six months later, the mass recurred, and she was referred to our hospital. Clinical evaluation, tru-cut biopsy, chest X-ray, and liver ultrasound were performed. Immunohistochemistry (ER, PR, and HER2) was unavailable. Surgical management consisted of modified radical mastectomy with axillary lymphadenectomy Histology confirmed invasive metaplastic carcinoma (13 × 7 × 5 cm) with vascular invasion and 4/19 lymph nodes positive for metastasis (T3N2Mx). Margins, skin, and nipple were free. Postoperative recovery was uneventful. The patient’s socioeconomic conditions prevented referral to a specialized cancer centre or access to systemic therapies This case illustrates the compounded challenges of managing rare, aggressive cancers in resource-limited settings. While MBC is inherently resistant to conventional therapies, the absence of specialized oncology centers, financial constraints, and lack of access to modern systemic treatments severely compromise outcomes in Tanzania
Conclusion: Metaplastic breast carcinoma is rare and aggressive, requiring specialized diagnostic and therapeutic approaches. In Tanzania, poverty and inadequate oncologic infrastructure make optimal management nearly impossible. This case underscores the urgent need for improved diagnostic capacity, affordable access to modern therapies, and the establishment of regional oncology centers to ensure equitable cancer care in low-resource countries
Feasibility, Acceptability and Effectiveness of Simulated Laughter Therapy on Depression among MDR-TB Patients in Northern Tanzania
Neema Makupa · Nurse · KCMC
Co-authors: Michael Burke
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Tuberculosis (TB) remains a major public health challenge worldwide, compounded by rising multidrug-resistant (MDR-TB), and extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis (XDR-TB) strains. Patients with multidrug-resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB) often experience significant psychological distress, particularly depression, due to prolonged hospitalization and lengthy treatment regimens and persistence of tuberculosis symptoms. Simulated laughter therapy is a non-pharmacological intervention that has been shown to improve psychological well-being mainly depression, physical symptoms, and overall quality of life.
Objectives: This study aims to assess the feasibility, acceptability and effectiveness of simulated laughter therapy on depression among MDR-TB patients hospitalized at Kibong’oto infectious disease hospital
Methods: This study employed a pre and post quasi-experimental design. The study population included adult patients diagnosed with MDR-TB. A sample size of twenty participants were recruited using consecutive sampling. Pre-test questionnaires were administered at recruitment, mid-time assessment at four weeks and post-test questionnaires after 8 weeks. The participants received simulated laughter therapy twice weekly for 8 consecutive weeks. Data are be analyzed using descriptive and inferential statistics and thematic coding of focus group and in-depth interviews
Results: Findings from the study will inform the design of a larger scale trial and contribute to integrating mental health support into MDR-TB care
Conclusion: The mental health challenges of long term hospitalised patients with MDR-TB are burdensome. Non-pharmacological interventions can be explored as a contribution to reducing this burden
Disseminated Iatrogenic Upper Gastrointestinal Kaposi Sarcoma Following Prolonged Steroid Use in a Patient with Crohn’s Disease: A Case Report
Glory Makupa · Doctor · KCMC
Co-authors: Furaha Serventi, Leila Mwakipunda, Godwin Nnko, Edwin Liwa, Salum Lidenge, and others
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Kaposi sarcoma is a malignant vascular neoplasm caused by human herpesvirus-8. It is mostly seen in immunocompromised individuals, particularly those with human immunodeficiency virus/acquired immunodeficiency syndrome or organ transplants. Cutaneous and mucosal Kaposi sarcoma manifestation is common; however, Kaposi sarcoma may involve the visceral organs. Iatrogenic or drug-induced Kaposi sarcoma may develop in immunocompromised patients undergoing immunosuppressive medication. It is quite uncommon for patients with inflammatory bowel diseases on immunosuppressive medication to develop intestinal Kaposi sarcoma This case report describes the clinical presentation of steroid-induced disseminated upper gastrointestinal Kaposi sarcoma in a patient with underlying Crohn’s disease and examines the management approach, associated challenges, and clinical outcome
Case Presentation: We report a rare case of iatrogenic Kaposi sarcoma in a 39 year old African male patient with Crohn’s disease undergoing long-term corticosteroid treatment. Patient history, clinical examination, and investigations revealed mucosal Kaposi sarcoma with human herpes virus-8 positivity. This prompted discontinuation of corticosteroids with subsequent transition to infliximab, a biologic agent. During the course of treatment with infliximab, the patient presented with extensive Kaposi sarcoma and was started on systemic chemotherapy with paclitaxel after multidisciplinary review. He completed six cycles with good tolerance and marked clinical response. At 6-month follow-up, he remained disease-free with improved gastrointestinal symptoms and overall clinical status
Conclusion: This case underscores the importance of maintaining a high index of suspicion for Kaposi sarcoma in patients on chronic immunosuppressive therapy who present with unusual skin or mucosal lesions
Kawasaki Disease in African Children:A Case Series
Mayila Mbuki · Doctor · AKU
Co-authors: Dr Deogratius Nkya
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Kawasaki disease (KD) is an acute pediatric vasculitis and a leading cause of acquired heart disease in children. Although traditionally associated with Asian populations, emerging reports suggest increasing recognition in Sub-Saharan Africa, where it remains underdiagnosed due to overlap with infectious diseases
Case Presentation: We report three African pediatric cases (two aged 2 years and one aged 6 years) presenting with prolonged fever unresponsive to antibiotics. All demonstrated classical mucocutaneous features including hyperaemic/cracked lips, polymorphous rash, non-purulent conjunctivitis, and strawberry tongue. Additional findings included extremity changes (periungual desquamation, edema) and cervical lymphadenopathy. Initial misdiagnoses included sepsis, Stevens–Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis, and deep neck infection Laboratory evaluation showed elevated inflammatory markers (CRP), leukocytosis, and progressive thrombocytosis with sterile blood cultures. One patient developed coronary artery ectasia on echocardiography, while others had normal cardiac findings. All patients received intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) and high-dose aspirin, resulting in rapid resolution of fever and improvement in inflammatory markers. Follow-up demonstrated favorable clinical outcomes and normalization or improvement of cardiac findings
Conclusion: KD is increasingly recognized in African pediatric populations but remains underdiagnosed. Persistent fever beyond five days with mucocutaneous features and rising inflammatory markers should raise suspicion. Early diagnosis and prompt treatment with IVIG and aspirin are essential to prevent cardiovascular complications. Greater clinician awareness and further epidemiological studies are needed to define disease burden in Sub-Saharan Africa
Acute Postpartum Respiratory Distress With Right Ventricular Dysfunction Complicating Into Postpartum Cardiomyopathy: A Case of Suspected Atypical Amniotic Fluid Embolism
Aniceth Muchunguzi · Doctor · AKH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE) is a devastating and unpredictable obstetric emergency typically characterized by sudden cardiopulmonary collapse and coagulopathy during or shortly after delivery. It is triggered by an anaphylactoid-histamine-mediated systemic response, evidently with no available confirmatory tests in living patients. AFE remains a diagnosis of exclusion, and atypical or incomplete presentations frequently overlap with other postpartum cardiopulmonary conditions, particularly peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM), stress-induced cardiomyopathy, and acute pulmonary edema. This overlap necessitates cautious interpretation and multidisciplinary evaluation
Case Presentation: We describe a postpartum case presenting with acute respiratory distress and right ventricular dysfunction, suggesting the possibility of atypical AFE versus unmasked PPCM. Pulmonary embolism was excluded on CT Pulmonary angiography, while echocardiography revealed right heart strain with biventricular dysfunction and pulmonary hypertension The patient was successfully managed with non-invasive ventilation, and pharmacologically in terms of pulmonary vasodilators, and diuretics—thus avoiding invasive mechanical ventilation or Extra-corporeal Membranous Oxygenation (ECMO). The patient improved with resolution of respiratory distress and weaning off oxygen support. She was transferred out of the ICU after 2 days and discharged home 2 days later At 10 weeks postpartum, repeat echocardiography demonstrated persistent left ventricular systolic dysfunction with LVEF 36%, fulfilling diagnostic criteria for peripartum cardiomyopathy. She remains on guideline-directed heart failure therapy with ongoing cardiology follow-up
Conclusion: This case highlights the diagnostic complexity of postpartum cardiopulmonary collapse, cautious diagnostic attribution, echocardiography-guided supportive management, multidisciplinary critical care and vigilant surveillance following unexplained postpartum respiratory failure. Future studies and registries are warranted to better define and understand the interplay between delayed/atypical AFE and PPCM
Assessment of Adherence to Self-Monitoring of Blood Glucose in Children and Adolescents with Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus at Mwananyamala Regional Referral Hospital in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
Khadija Feruz · Intern · AKH
Co-authors: Dr. Leonard J. Malasa
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) affects children and adolescents globally, requiring regular self-monitoring of blood glucose (SMBG) for effective management. However, adherence to SMBG among young patients in Tanzania remains under-examined
Objectives: To assess adherence to self-monitoring of blood glucose in children and adolescents with T1DM at Mwananyamala Regional Referral Hospital from September to November 2024
Methods: This descriptive cross-sectional study included children and adolescents with T1DM and their caregivers. Data were collected via questionnaires and analysed using SPSS Version 23
Results: Although 90.7% of 140 participants had access to glucometers, only 50.7% adhered to the recommended SMBG frequency of 3-4 times daily. Factors influencing adherence included caregiver education level and training on glucometer use
Conclusion: Despite high glucometer access, adherence varies, highlighting the need for targeted training and caregiver support to improve adherence
Knowledge, Attitudes and Practices Regarding Oral Malodor among Outpatients Attending Mbeya Zonal Referral Hospital, Tanzania: A Cross sectional study
Shani Omari · Intern · AKH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Oral malodor (halitosis) is a prevalent condition affecting 22-50% of populations globally. Despite high prevalence, it remains under recognized as a public health concern in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs). In Tanzania, self-reported prevalence ranges from 44% in hospital settings to 72% among adolescents, yet localized data on knowledge, attitudes, and preventive practices remain scarce. Understanding these determinants is essential for designing targeted health interventions in resource-limited settings
Objectives: To assess the knowledge, attitudes, and practices (KAP) regarding oral malodor among outpatients attending Mbeya Zonal Referral Hospital, and identify factors associated with good preventive practices
Methods: A hospital-based cross-sectional study was conducted among 204 outpatients at Mbeya Zonal Referral Hospital (February-April 2026). Data were collected via structured questionnaires administered electronically through KoboToolbox, covering sociodemographic characteristics, knowledge about malodor causes and management (7-item scale), attitudes toward psychosocial impact (5-item Likert scale), and oral hygiene practices (6-item checklist). Good knowledge, attitudes, and practices were defined as >=60% on respective scales. Binary logistic regression identified independent predictors of good practices. Data were analyzed using SPSS 25
Results: Mean age of respondents was 40.4 years (SD +/- 17.4); 52.0% were male, 61.8% urban residents. Knowledge was severely limited: 89.2% demonstrated poor knowledge (mean 35.8% accuracy), despite 95.6% recognizing poor oral hygiene as a cause. Attitudes were substantially higher: 92.2% expressed high concern about malodor's psychosocial impact, with 94.1% acknowledging effects on self-esteem and 93.1% on social life. Practices were predominantly poor: only 11.3% engaged in good practices; whilst tooth brushing was universal (98.5%), supplementary practices were rare - tongue scraping 28.9%, flossing 1.0%, preventive dental visits 3.4%. Binary logistic regression identified knowledge (OR=1.048 per 1% increase, p=0.001) and education level (OR=3.136 per level, p<0.001) as significant independent predictors of good practices. Notably, attitudes did not significantly predict practices (p=0.944), revealing a critical attitude-practice gap
Conclusion: This study documents a severe knowledge-attitude-practice gap regarding oral malodor in a Tanzanian referral hospital setting. Whilst concern about malodor's psychosocial consequences is nearly universal, structural barriers - cost of preventive care, limited access to dental services, scarcity of dental workforce, and absence of integrated oral health education - prevent translation of intention into action. Knowledge and education independently predict practice adoption, suggesting that effective interventions must combine knowledge translation with removal of structural barriers. Recommendations include integrating malodor prevention into primary care and school curricula, task-shifting oral health education to community health workers, and reducing cost barriers to preventive care
Exploring Experiences of Online Gender-Based Violence among Female Undergraduate University Students in Dar-es-Salaam
Lorraine David · Intern · AKH
Tap to read full abstract ▼
Background: Online gender violence (OGBV) is now a threat globally, primarily among female university students who engage heavily in the internet. OGBV includes cyberstalking, cyber harassment, and involuntary posting of indecent photos or videos, which have serious psychological, social, and academic impacts on the survivors. Despite increasing internet penetration in Tanzania, there is limited research documenting the lived experiences of female undergraduate students, highlighting a critical knowledge gap
Objectives: The study aimed to explore the experiences of Online Gender-Based Violence (OGBV) among female undergraduate university students in Tanzania, specifically focusing on common forms, contributing factors, coping mechanisms, and institutional responses
Methods: This exploratory qualitative study was conducted among female undergraduate students in universities, student leaders and university officials in Dar es Salaam (MUHAS and KIUT). It employed purposive sampling, using 16 Key Informant Interviews (KIIs), two Focus Group Discussions (FGDs), and a policy document review. Data were analysed using thematic analysis
Results: The study established five common forms of OGBV: cyberbullying, image-based sexual violence, unwanted sexual messages, sexist hate speech, and doxing. Students employed individual coping mechanisms such as staying offline, receiving emotional support from friends and family members, and reporting to university authorities. Institutional support systems, such as peer counselling and gender desks, were found to be variably known, underused, and perceived as ineffective
Conclusion: OGBV is a highly neglected but far-reaching threat to the physical security, mental health, and academic participation of Tanzanian university female students. Though students show individual resilience, institutional responses are weak, poorly publicized, and confined to offline violence
Recommendation: Prevention of OGBV demands a multi-pronged approach with digital safety training, student-centered policies, further awareness campaigns, and enhanced institutional dedication towards the protection of students in both physical and online spaces
